title: Symbols, predicates and rules

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Symbols, predicates and rules; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Knowledge Representation and Reasoning plus Reasoning Under Uncertainty; atomic topic: Symbols, predicates and rules
sources.claim: A symbolic knowledge base uses explicit constants, predicates, relations, facts, rules, and queries whose meaning depends on the declared domain and closed- or open-world assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A symbolic knowledge base uses explicit constants, predicates, relations, facts, rules, and queries whose meaning depends on the declared domain and closed- or open-world assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Facts parent(ana,bo) and a rule parent(x,y) -> ancestor(x,y) support one bounded ancestor query under named assumptions. Your goal is to create and evaluate a small symbolic knowledge base while stating closed- or open-world assumptions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: AI problems and agents; Problem and environment characteristics; pf data records. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A symbolic knowledge base uses explicit constants, predicates, relations, facts, rules, and queries whose meaning depends on the declared domain and closed- or open-world assumptions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Natural-language ambiguity is not removed automatically, absence can mean false or unknown, inconsistent rules can derive conflicts, names need identity assumptions, and symbolic truth does not prove the world is modeled completely. The learner must reject this overclaim: If a fact is not stored in a symbolic knowledge base, it is false in every reasoning system.

includes: Translate a bounded natural-language domain into facts, relations, rules and queries.
Create and evaluate a small symbolic knowledge base while stating closed- or open-world assumptions.
excludes: Natural-language ambiguity is not removed automatically, absence can mean false or unknown, inconsistent rules can derive conflicts, names need identity assumptions, and symbolic truth does not prove the world is modeled completely.

outcomes: Create and evaluate a small symbolic knowledge base while stating closed- or open-world assumptions.

examples.title: Symbols, predicates and rules: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Facts parent(ana,bo) and a rule parent(x,y) -> ancestor(x,y) support one bounded ancestor query under named assumptions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Facts parent(ana,bo) and a rule parent(x,y) -> ancestor(x,y) support one bounded ancestor query under named assumptions.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Declare the vocabulary, domain, identity, and open- or closed-world policy; distinguish explicit negation, contradiction, and missing knowledge.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Declare the vocabulary, domain, identity, and open- or closed-world policy; distinguish explicit negation, contradiction, and missing knowledge.

examples.title: Symbols, predicates and rules: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: The database lacks vaccinated(bo); an open-world policy returns unknown rather than concluding not vaccinated.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: The database lacks vaccinated(bo); an open-world policy returns unknown rather than concluding not vaccinated.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Natural-language ambiguity is not removed automatically, absence can mean false or unknown, inconsistent rules can derive conflicts, names need identity assumptions, and symbolic truth does not prove the world is modeled completely.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: If a fact is not stored in a symbolic knowledge base, it is false in every reasoning system.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create and evaluate a small symbolic knowledge base while stating closed- or open-world assumptions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If a fact is not stored in a symbolic knowledge base, it is false in every reasoning system.
misconceptions.correction: Declare the vocabulary, domain, identity, and open- or closed-world policy; distinguish explicit negation, contradiction, and missing knowledge.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: If a fact is not stored in a symbolic knowledge base, it is false in every reasoning system.

analogy.example: an evidence board that distinguishes explicit facts, supported rule chains, missing knowledge, probabilities, and dependency assumptions can introduce Symbols, predicates and rules only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: symbolic representation, inference, Bayesian updating, and graphical dependencies
limitations: encoded facts can be incomplete or inconsistent and probabilistic edges do not automatically establish causation

recap.summary: You should now be able to create and evaluate a small symbolic knowledge base while stating closed- or open-world assumptions. Keep this boundary in view: Natural-language ambiguity is not removed automatically, absence can mean false or unknown, inconsistent rules can derive conflicts, names need identity assumptions, and symbolic truth does not prove the world is modeled completely.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Symbols, predicates and rules with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: If a fact is not stored in a symbolic knowledge base, it is false in every reasoning system.
Apply the boundary to: The database lacks vaccinated(bo); an open-world policy returns unknown rather than concluding not vaccinated.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Logical inference and consistency”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
